revision.text: #redirect[[en:Sashikuni_Ōkami]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''刺国大神'''（さしくにおおかみ）は、[[日本神話]]の[[神]]。
==概要== 古事記にのみ登場。その神格、出身などどういった神なのかは不明。
==系譜== [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主大神]]を産んでいる。
==関連項目== * [[刺国若比売]] * [[天之冬衣神]] * [[大国主]]

{{出典の明記|date=2017-08-07}} '''刺国大神'''（さしくにおおかみ）は、[[日本神話]]の[[神]]。
==概要== 古事記にのみ登場。その神格、出身などどういった神なのかは不明。
[[古事記伝]]において刺国とは、「刺す」=占有すると考え、刺国大神の名義は国を占有する神の意とされている。
==系譜== [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主大神]]を産んでいる。

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 刺国大神 | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 刺国大神 | 別名 = 佐斯久斯布刀比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->国土神 | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[刺国若比売]] | 宮 = | 神社 = }} '''刺国大神'''（さしくにおおのかみ）は、[[日本神話]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]。
==概要== 古事記にのみ登場。その神格、出身などどういった神なのかは不明。
[[古事記伝]]において刺国とは、「刺す」=占有すると考え、刺国大神の名義は国を占有する神の意とされている。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主大神]]を産んでいる。

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 刺国大神 | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 刺国大神 | 別名 = 佐斯久斯布刀比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->国土神 | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[刺国若比売]] | 宮 = | 神社 = }} '''刺国大神'''（さしくにおおのかみ）は、[[日本神話]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]。
==概要== 『[[古事記]]』では'''刺国大神'''、[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元気記]]』では'''佐斯久斯布刀比売'''（さしくしふとひめ）と表記される。
『古事記』においは[[刺国若比売]]の親（父）として名前のみ登場。
刺国大神の「刺す」は「標（しめ）」を刺すこと、占有すること、「大」は刺国若比売に対応する親の意と解し、名義は「国を占有する親」と考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。
==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主大神]]を産んでいる。
==祀る神社== * [[刺田比古神社]] [[本居宣長]]が古来の祭神として考えた
==関連項目== * [[刺国若比売]] * [[天之冬衣神]] * [[大国主]] {{デフォルトソート:さしくにおおかみ}} [[category:日本の神]] [[Category:国津神]]

==系譜== [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主大神]]を産んでいる。

revision.text:
{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 刺国大神 | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 刺国大神（サシクニオオカミ） | 別名 = 佐斯久斯布刀比売 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->国土神 | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[刺国若比売]] | 宮 = | 神社 = }} '''刺国大神'''（佐斯久斯布刀比売）は、[[日本神話]]の[[神 (神道)|神]]。 == 概要 == 『[[古事記]]』では'''刺国大神'''、[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元気記]]』では'''佐斯久斯布刀比売'''（さしくしふとひめ）と表記される。
『古事記』においは[[刺国若比売]]の親（父）として名前のみ登場。
刺国大神の「刺す」は「標（しめ）」を刺すこと、占有すること、「大」は刺国若比売に対応する親の意と解し、名義は「国を占有する親」と考えられる<ref>新潮日本古典集成 古事記</ref>。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] [[刺国若比売]]の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子の[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主神]]を産んでいる。 == 祀る神社 == * [[刺田比古神社]]（和歌山県和歌山市片岡町） : [[本居宣長]]が古来の祭神として考えた。 == 参考文献 == {{Reflist}} == 関連項目 == * [[刺国若比売]] * [[天之冬衣神]] * [[大国主]] {{日本神話}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さしくにおおかみ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:十七世神]]

{{基礎情報 日本の神 | 名 = 刺国大神 | 神祇 = <!-- 天神、地祇、天津神、国津神など -->[[国津神]] | 全名 = 刺国大神（サシクニオオノカミ） | 別名 = 刺國大神、佐志久斯布刀比売、佐志久斯布刀比賣 | 別称 = | 神階 = | 神格 = <!-- 太陽、月、山、海など司るもの -->国土神 | 陵所 = | 父 = | 母 = | 親 = <!-- まぐあいによって生まれない場合 --> | 配偶者 = | 子 = [[刺国若比売]] | 宮 = | 神社 = | 記紀等 = [[古事記]] }} '''刺国大神'''（サシクニオオノカミ／サシクニオオカミ、{{smaller|[[歴史的仮名遣]]：}}サシクニオホノカミ／サシクニオホカミ）は、[[日本神話]]に登場する[[神 (神道)|神]]。'''サシクシフトヒメ'''（佐志久斯布刀比売、佐志久斯布刀比賣）ともする。 == 概要 == 『古事記』では刺国大神、[[粟鹿神社]]の書物『[[粟鹿大明神元記]]』では佐志久斯布刀比売と表記される。
『古事記』においは刺国若比売の親（父）として名前のみ登場。
刺国大神の「刺す」は「標（しめ）」を刺すこと、占有すること、「大」は刺国若比売に対応する親の意と解し、名義は「国を占有する親」と考えられる{{Sfn|西宮|2014|p=373}}。 == 系譜 == [[ファイル:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|須佐之男命から大国主神までの系図（『古事記』による）。青は男神、赤は女神]] 刺国若比売の父とされており、娘は[[淤美豆奴神]]の子の[[天之冬衣神]]と[[大国主|大国主神]]を産んでいる。 == 祀る神社 == * [[刺田比古神社]]（[[和歌山県]][[和歌山市]]片岡町） - [[本居宣長]]が古来の祭神として考えた。 == 注釈 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * <!-- にしみや -->{{Cite book|和書 |title=古事記 |volume= |date=2014年10月30日 |publisher=[[新潮社]] |ref={{SfnRef|西宮|2014}} |author=[[西宮一民]] 校注 |isbn=978-4-10-620801-0 |series=[[新潮日本古典集成]]（新装版）}} == 関連項目 == * [[十七世神]] == 外部リンク == * [https://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/sashikunionokami/ 刺国大神 – 國學院大學 古典文化学事業] {{日本神話}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さしくにおおのかみ}} [[Category:国津神]] [[Category:日本の神]] [[Category:十七世神]]

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈
| Other Names =

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

}}
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity

| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami

| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈
| Other Names =

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈
