'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the [[:en:Izumo Province|Izumo Province]].<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]]

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a [[:en:Deity|Deity]] from [[:en:Japanese mythology|Japanese mythology]]. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to [[:en:Ame-no-Fuyukinu|Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] and [[:en:Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninushi]] through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, [[:en:Dōsojin|Michikaeshi]] (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]], [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ojin]] and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}}

}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

==Family tree==

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| children = {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}
| father = 
| consort = 
| mother = 
|cult_centre={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| offspring = {{ill|Q38276815|WD=Q38276815}}
}}
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}}

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}}

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo}}

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| children = {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}
| father = 
| consort = 
| mother = 
|cult_centre={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}
| offspring = {{ill|Q38276815|WD=Q38276815}}
}}
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}}

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈
| Other Names = 
| Rank        = 
| Domain      = Land deity
| Burial Site = 
| Father      = 
| Mother      = 
| Parents     = 
| Spouse      = 
| Children    = {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}
| Shrine      = 
| Appears In  = {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}
}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo}}

== Shrines Dedicated to Sasikuni Ōkami ==
* {{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasihiko Shrine|qq=}} ({{ill|Kataoka Town|ja|片岡町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kataoka Town|1=Kataoka Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037300}}, {{ill|Wakayama (city)|en|Wakayama (city)|WD=Q200747|lt=Wakayama City}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}) – The ancient deity of the shrine, according to {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|ja|本屣宣長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Motoori Norinaga|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo}}

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| children = {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}
| father = 
| consort = 
| mother = 
|cult_centre={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}|cult_center={{ill|Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasihiko Shrine|lt=Sasadahiko Shrine|ja|刺田比古神社|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasadahiko Shrine|qq=}}| gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}
| offspring = {{ill|Q38276815|WD=Q38276815|1=Sashikuni Wakahime}}
}}
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}}

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}}

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}}

==Overview==

{{Infobox Japanese deity
| Name        = Sasikuni Ōkami
| Deity Type  = {{ill|Kunitsukami|ja|国津神|1=Kunitsukami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|comment=confirming this goes to Kunitsukami|1=Kunitsukami|2=zh|3=國神|4=fr|5=Kunitsukami|6=mni|7=ꯀꯨꯅꯤꯠꯁꯨꯀꯃꯤ}}
| Full Name   = 刺国大神 (Sasikuni ōno kami)
| Alternate Names = 刺国大神, 佐志久斯布刀比士, 佐志久斯布刀比贈

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}

==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo}}

| offspring = {{ill|Q38276815|WD=Q38276815|1=Sashikuni Wakahime}}
}}
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} (刺国大神) was a Japanese deity associated with {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qq=}} and the {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo Province|1=Izumo Province}}.<ref name="fountainhead" />{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0" />

He's the father of {{ill|Sashikuni Wakahime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|en:draft|simple|User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}<ref name="fountainhead">{{cite book |last=Herbert |first=J. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sg_JBQAAQBAJ&pg=PT402 |title=Shinto: At the Fountainhead of Japan |publisher=Taylor & Francis |year=2010 |isbn=978-1-136-90376-2 |series=Routledge Library Editions: Japan |page=402 |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref>{{rp|278}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=Sashikuniō • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/sashikunio/|access-date=2024-03-22|website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史|language=en-GB}}</ref>.

The Sashikuni in his name refers to land marking.<ref name=":0" /> This makes him analogous to {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}}

==Overview==

'''Sasikuni Ōkami''' (刺国大神/サシクニオオノカミ, {{smaller|[[Historical Kana Usage|historical kana]]:}} サシクニオホノカミ) is a {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=Deity|1=Deity}} from {{ill|Japanese mythology|en|Japanese mythology|WD=Q814158|lt=Japanese mythology|1=Japanese mythology}}. The deity is also referred to as '''Sasikushifutohime''' (佐志久斯布刀比士).

In the *Kojiki*, Sasikuni Ōkami is mentioned by name as the parent (father) of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. In the records of {{ill|Awaga Shrine|ja|米鵠神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Awaga Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (*Awaga Daimyōjin Genki*), the deity is referred to as Sasikushifutohime.

The name "Sasu" (刺す) is interpreted as "marking" or "staking out" a boundary, signifying ownership. "Ō" (大) denotes the status of being a parent in relation to {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}. Thus, the name is understood to mean "the parent who claims the land"{{Sfn|Nishinomiya|2014|p=373}}.

== Genealogy ==
[[File:Susanowo family tree.png|thumb|250px|The genealogy from {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|lt=Susanoo|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之田生命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo|13=User:Immanuelle/Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} as depicted in the *Kojiki*. Male deities are shown in blue, and female deities in red.]]
Sasikuni Ōkami is identified as the father of {{ill|Sasikuni Wakabime|ja|刺国若比売|1=Sashikuni Wakahime|2=ja|3=刺国若比売|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasikuni Wakabime|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Wakahime|qq=}}, who gave birth to {{ill|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|en|Ame-no-Fuyukinu|WD=Q48744862|lt=Ame-no-Fuyukinu|1=Ame-no-Fuyukinu}} and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}} through {{ill|Omizunu no Kami|ja|水童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omizunu no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Title ==
{{ill|Sashikuni Ōkami|lt=Sashikuni Ōkami|ja|刺国大神|en:draft|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni_Okami|8=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sashikuni Ōkami|qq=}} has the distinction of being one of only seven {{ill|kami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kami|qq=}} to be honored with the title '''Ōkami''' ({{CJKV|j=大神|qq=draft}}) or "Great Kami"; the other six are [[Izanagi|Izanagi]], {{ill|Izanami|lt=Izanami|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izanami|qq=}}, {{ill|Dōsojin|en|Dōsojin|WD=Q269515|lt=Michikaeshi|1=Dōsojin}} (also known as Yomido ni sayarimasu ōkami (?) who is the kami of the great rock used by Izanagi to obstruct the way to Yomi, and thus, preventing emergence of evil spirits from the Underworld), {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|en|4=fr|6=simple|7=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko Ōkami|qq=}}, {{ill|Inari Ōkami|lt=Inari|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inari Ōkami|qq=}}, and {{ill|Amaterasu|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|qq=}}. Sarutahiko and Inari appear to be the only Okami from the ''kunitsukami'', or earthly kami, the others being ''{{ill|Amatsukami|en|4=fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami|qq=}}'' (heavenly deities). Although there is some other Daimyōjin and Daigongen, most of them appeared later in the History of Japanese Religion, such as [[Hachiman]] (deification of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingu|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ojin|1=Emperor Ōjin}} and maybe [[Takenouchi no Sukune]], as a successful head of state) or Hindu deities, the meaning of Daimyōjin appear to be a little different from Ōkami, the latter being obviously more ancient. <ref>{{Cite web|title=Kami – OCCULT WORLD|url=https://occult-world.com/kami/|access-date=2024-03-19|language=en-US}}</ref>{{Better source}}
